papers.title: Tailoring Strength and Ductility of an Fe–Mn–Al–C Low-Density Duplex Steel by Controlling the Cooling Path after Hot Rolling
papers.paper_scope: Investigate the effect of post-hot-rolling cooling path (air cooling vs furnace cooling) and subsequent annealing at 800–1000 °C on the microstructure evolution and tensile properties of an Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C low-density duplex steel, with emphasis on austenite stability and strength-ductility synergy.

processes.description: Hot rolling followed by air cooling to ambient temperature.
processes.processes_notes: Cast ingot forged at 1050 °C, homogenized 2 h, reheated at 1200 °C for 2 h, hot rolled in 900–1100 °C range by seven passes to ~4 mm sheet, then air cooled.

processes.description: Hot rolling followed by furnace cooling to ambient temperature.
processes.processes_notes: Same thermomechanical processing as AC, but cooled in furnace after hot rolling.

processes.description: Hot rolling, air cooling, and annealing at 800 °C for 30 min.
Hot rolling, air cooling, and annealing at 900 °C for 30 min.

Hot rolling, furnace cooling, and annealing at 900 °C for 30 min.
Hot rolling, furnace cooling, and annealing at 1000 °C for 30 min.

structures.overall_structure: Banded morphology along rolling direction; alternating δ-ferrite and prior austenite with martensite.
Banded morphology along rolling direction; alternating δ-ferrite and prior austenite with minor martensite.
Duplex microstructure of austenite and ferrite with needle-like α-ferrite embedded in austenite bands; δ-ferrite present.
Duplex microstructure of austenite and ferrite with needle-like α-ferrite; δ-ferrite present.
Duplex microstructure of austenite and ferrite; equiaxed recrystallized austenite and recovered/partially recrystallized δ-ferrite.
Banded morphology composed of δ-ferrite and prior austenite with small amount of martensite; fine equiaxed α-ferrite formed.
Banded morphology composed of δ-ferrite and prior austenite with small amount of martensite; fine equiaxed α-ferrite.
Banded morphology composed of δ-ferrite and prior austenite with small amount of martensite; fine equiaxed α-ferrite.

defect_interaction.quality_description: Strain concentration at martensite-austenite interface nucleates cracks during deformation.

interface_notes.stress_strain_distribution: Strain localization at martensite-austenite interface leads to crack initiation and propagation.

interfaces.phase_evolution: Martensite forms during final cooling after annealing due to lower austenite stability caused by larger grain size.

interface_notes.stress_strain_distribution: Needle-like α-ferrite embedded in austenite bands refines austenite grain size and enhances austenite stability.

interfaces.phase_evolution: Prior martensite partly degenerates to needle-like α-ferrite during annealing, inheriting the prior martensite morphology.

characterization_methods.key_findings: Banded δ-ferrite and prior austenite alternately distributed; 26 vol% twin martensite; darker IQ region indicates martensite with large distortion.

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase mapping, grain boundary distribution and KAM analysis
characterization_methods.key_findings: Austenite fraction 42 vol%; KAM of austenite 0.48°; high-angle (>15°) and low-angle (>2°) grain boundaries in δ-ferrite.
Banded δ-ferrite and prior austenite; 2.8 vol% martensite; less martensite than AC due to slower cooling.

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase mapping, grain boundary distribution and KAM analysis
characterization_methods.key_findings: Austenite fraction 51 vol%; KAM of austenite 0.43°; low-angle grain boundaries in banded δ-ferrite.
Duplex austenite and ferrite; needle-like α-ferrite embedded in austenite; film austenite thickened between α-ferrite.
Austenite separated by needle-like α-ferrite; low-angle grain boundaries length in ferrite 4351.1 μm; average austenite grain size 3.5 μm.

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase fraction before and after tensile deformation
characterization_methods.key_findings: Austenite fraction 58.6% before deformation and 23.7% after deformation.
Duplex austenite and ferrite with needle-like α-ferrite; similar microstructure to AC-A800.
Low-angle grain boundaries length in ferrite 2685.4 μm; average austenite grain size 4.2 μm.

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase fraction before and after tensile deformation
characterization_methods.key_findings: Austenite fraction 61.4% before deformation and 44.4% after deformation.
Duplex austenite and ferrite; dominant reverse transformation of martensite; small fraction of α-ferrite.
Equiaxed recrystallized austenite; δ-ferrite fully recovered and partially recrystallized; low-angle grain boundaries length 1634.3 μm; average austenite grain size 5.8 μm.

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase fraction before and after tensile deformation
characterization_methods.key_findings: Austenite fraction 57.1% before deformation and 46.4% after deformation.

characterization_methods.purpose: Annealed microstructure and fracture surface observation
characterization_methods.key_findings: Banded δ-ferrite and prior austenite with small amount of martensite; fine equiaxed α-ferrite; fracture surface shows cleavage and cracking.
Banded microstructure with larger austenite grains; average austenite grain size 9.3 μm; darker IQ maps indicate large distortion.

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase fraction before and after tensile deformation
characterization_methods.key_findings: Austenite fraction 62% before deformation and 56.5% after deformation.
Banded δ-ferrite and prior austenite with small amount of martensite; fine equiaxed α-ferrite.
Average austenite grain size 8.1 μm; banded microstructure retained.

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase fraction before and after tensile deformation
characterization_methods.key_findings: Austenite fraction 58.6% before deformation and 52.4% after deformation.
Banded δ-ferrite and prior austenite with small amount of martensite; fine equiaxed α-ferrite.
Average austenite grain size 10.1 μm; banded microstructure with large distortion in IQ maps.

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase fraction before and after tensile deformation
characterization_methods.key_findings: Austenite fraction 60.6% before deformation and 52.1% after deformation.

computational_details.validation_against_experiment: Calculated Ms_lim values support experimental observation that almost no martensite forms during final cooling of air-cooled annealed samples.

analytical_model.model_name: Martensite start temperature (Ms) equation with grain size term
analytical_model.equation_role: Estimate upper limit of martensite start temperature to assess austenite stability
analytical_model.parameters: B = 613 μm·K^-1; V_γ is austenite grain volume; w_Mn, w_Al, w_C are mass percents
k: Hall-Petch coefficient; d: grain size
M: Taylor factor; α: 0.2 (for fcc); G: shear modulus; b: Burgers vector; ρ: dislocation density
analytical_model.model_note: Δσ_d = M α G b ρ^(1/2)

unmapped_findings: Figure 5 (tabular XRD data) reports austenite fractions before and after deformation: AC-A800 58.6%→23.7%, AC-A900 61.4%→44.4%, AC-A1000 57.1%→46.4%, FC-A800 62%→56.5%, FC-A900 58.6%→52.4%, FC-A1000 60.6%→52.1%, indicating pronounced TRIP effect in air-cooled annealed samples.

Figure 9 provides tabular engineering stress–strain data for all hot-rolled and annealed samples; air-cooled annealed samples achieve elongations of 56.9%, 39.5%, and 31.1%, whereas furnace-cooled annealed samples achieve only 19.0%, 15.4%, and 10.6%.

Figure 10 provides tabular true stress–strain and work hardening rate data, showing an extra Stage III work hardening rate recovery in air-cooled annealed samples and abrupt fracture without necking in furnace-cooled annealed samples.
The low-angle grain boundary lengths in ferrite measured by EBSD are 4351.1 μm (AC-A800), 2685.4 μm (AC-A900), and 1634.3 μm (AC-A1000), indicating progressive recovery during annealing.

Fracture surface of AC-A800 exhibits dimples indicative of ductile fracture, while FC-A800 exhibits cleavage-dominated brittle fracture with cracking at martensite–austenite interfaces and delamination of banded microstructure.
The austenite grain size distributions in Figure 7 show that furnace-cooled annealed samples have much larger austenite grains than air-cooled annealed samples at all annealing temperatures.
